comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses single-fact question answering over large-scale KBs by proposing CFO: a conditional factorization p(s,r|q)=p(r|q)p(s|q,r) implemented with neural networks, combined with a focused pruning method that uses a sequence-labeling (BiGRU-CRF) model to find the most likely subject mention. The paper also studies entity representations (TransE pretraining and binary type vectors). On the large SIMPLEQUESTIONS / FB5M benchmark, CFO attains 75.7% top-1 accuracy, substantially improving over prior work.

Major strengths:
- Clear, well-motivated decomposition: inferring relation first (limited set) then subject given relation is sensible for large KBs.
- Focused pruning is a simple, effective idea: steering candidate generation to a single most probable mention reduces noise and yields large gains; authors provide analysis showing why (more single-candidate cases).
- Thorough experimental evaluation on a large dataset; comparisons with reasonable baselines (embedding avg, LTG-CNN, memory networks) and ablations (pruning methods, entity representations, relation/subject encoders). Reported improvement (≈+11.8%) is substantial.
- Practical considerations addressed: use of pretrained GloVe, TransE, negative-sampling hinge losses, and an efficient type-vector representation when supervision is sparse.

Major weaknesses / concerns:
- Reliance on KB type coverage and entity linking: the strong performance of binary type vectors suggests the method depends on good type annotations and on ability to match mention strings to KB aliases. Real KBs with sparse or noisy type info or more ambiguous entity mentions may reduce effectiveness. The paper acknowledges some of this but more analysis of failure cases would help.
- Focused pruning keeps only the single most probable mention. This is effective on SIMPLEQUESTIONS but could be brittle when questions contain multiple possible mentions, nested mentions, or require coreference/discontiguous mentions. The approach’s robustness to such cases is not tested.
- The subject-relation compatibility term h(r,s) is an indicator (s→r). While simple and fast, richer parametrizations could better disambiguate entities sharing types; the paper notes this but leaves exploration to future work.
- Limited discussion of scalability/runtime at inference time (e.g., cost of querying KB aliases or using Freebase suggest API) and how negative sampling sizes (1024) affect training time. Some practical details are present but more profiling would be useful for practitioners.

Suggestions for authors:
- Include an analysis of failure modes: examples where focused pruning chooses the wrong mention or where type vectors are insufficient. This will clarify limits of the approach.
- Evaluate robustness to noisier KBs or to questions requiring multi-span mentions (if available), or at least discuss how the method could be extended (e.g., allow top-k mentions rather than k=1).
- Consider a learned subject-relation compatibility model (beyond indicator) and report whether joint training of p(r|q) and p(s|q,r) helps when such coupling exists.
- Provide some runtime/memory numbers for training and inference to guide practical deployment.

Minor comments:
- The paper is generally well-written and experiments are adequate. Some notation could be tightened (explicitly stating when parameters are shared or not). The method and results merit publication, and the focused pruning is an idea likely to be useful in related QA work.
